The runway theme was 'She-Vil Villain'

item.title: I wish that President Biden had been elected earlier. I really liked him.
item.description: He had many faults, but so do I. I just think this was a good line. I hope he's well. Thank you for your time.

item.dc:creator: JamieSays

item.title: The Big Behemoth of 1911 has finally been restored!
item.description:
Me right now: https://i.imgur.com/QSilqA6.mp4 Where to even begin? For those who have been following this restoration, I think we can all agree that it's been an absolute journey. For those who are wondering why someone would post a picture of a crusty, dirty, semi-nude cash register, now's a good a time as any for a recap! So. Way back in the distant year of 2025, I lucked upon an absolute skanker of a cash register in need of saving. Built by the National Cash Register Company in 1911, it's seen a lot of use and a ton of abuse. It was purchased by two brothers for use in their hardware store in Fairbanks, Iowa and was a fixture of that business even after they eventually sold the store to another local family. The register continued to be used up through the 1970s when, presumably, it was decommissioned after the original building was demolished and rebuilt to allow for a more spacious, modern hardware store. Since then, time and disuse caused the cash register to languish in storage, where pieces of it gradually went missing and the internals dried out and seized. I've spent the better part of three straight months working to restore this bit of history, beginning way back in December (https://imgur.com/gallery/wC0UGjE). I figured it would make for a nice, relaxing winter project. I'm unsure if I could really call it nice or relaxing now that it's done, but it was a major learning experience for me and I'd be lying if I didn't say it was at least as fun as it was painful. :D My last update was two weeks ago (https://imgur.com/gallery/V22G4ak) where I featured the maple syrup evaporator I'd just added to my workflow and repurposed as a heated bath for the brass I had just finished polishing. Time to finally show you all how that went!
Onto the restoration! After getting everything laid out and ready to go, I filled the new basin and ran it up to as hot as my immersion heater (the suspicious rod in the middle) would get it. Turns out it can run the temperature up to just shy of 120F, which is perfect for me since the polishing compound I need to get off melts at just below 100F.
Throw in some Dawn degreasing soap to help lift up the polish, which uses grease as a binder.
And after soaking the first batch of panels for about an hour, the bath has already turned murky. A good sign!
It occurred to me halfway through the process that, hey, dummy, you should take some before and after photos. So, here's the before of the breastplate still caked in polishing schmutz...
And the after, once I'd brushed off all the loose compound. To say this was easy is an understatement. The polish basically just melted off the metal and only needed to be lightly brushed away, which beats the pants off my old method of scrubbing the hell out of the metal in my utility sink!
Here's an example of what the polish does after simmering for a good hour. It basically just turns into loose sludge. Tenouttaten, would soak again!
And while it looks gross, it's an absolute game changer for me. So much of the time I used to spend with these registers was just sitting at my basement sink scrubbing, getting sore, and hoping all the physical effort would be enough so I wouldn't have to do it again. There's nothing more disheartening than exhausting yourself, only to realize you missed a spot and have to lug heavy panels back downstairs to try again.
With a heated basin, that problem is SOLVED.
Seriously, I'm so thankful to those of you who helped me pay for the basin. It's taken so much of the pain out of the brass process, and I actually feel like I'm doing this the right way for a change. You guys are all rock stars. <3
(Ignore the spill, someone forgot to tell Ellie to put the gasket in the hose before threading it onto the drain valve.) Something I did notice as I brought out each cleaned piece was that the metal was starting to develop a filmy texture as it dried.
Sure enough, as the minutes passed while I took some snapshots of the washed brass, the fogging started to become hard to ignore.
It didn't take me very long to work out what was wrong, and thankfully it was just a rookie mistake I could fix once I swallowed my pride. It turns out I got a little too excited about how quick and easy the polishing compound was coming off, and I sort of forgot a key step in the process. Rinsing. Ellie, you forgot RINSING.
Yeah, not exactly what I'd call clean dishwater. In my defense, I was alternating between washing brass and trying to unbury myself from the blizzard that was actively dumping more than a yard of snow on my hometown. I'm allowed to have poobrain! :D
So, as I moved all the brass off the workbench and set up the basin a second time - this time with the intention to make a pass by the utility sink for the mandatory rinse - I had a bit of a brainy moment. Looking at all the metal, I could see the patina was all over the place thanks to the previous owner's attempts at wiping it down with cleaners, letting it degrade in damp conditions, and generally just not treating it well. Add to the fact that I'd washed each piece and gone at them with a buffing wheel, the final effect was a very shiny but very uneven collection of brass. So I figured, I have a big bottle of this Flitz Brass Cleaner, some sturdy rubber gloves, and an organic respirator. Why not give this stuff a shot?
So. Yeah. Yeah, that'll absolutely work.
Less than a half hour of rinsing, spritzing, and re-rinsing later and every panel is looking choice shiny. The patinas across each piece has been completely reset so they can all re-darken evenly over the course of the next few years.
So shiny. So clean. So stoked.
Even the little guys that go around the keys came out looking golden!
And the crank-side panel, which usually always looks like a small disaster, looks squeaky clean!
I am a happy horse, yes I am.
Last money shot, and now that the brass is done, I'm extra motivated to get all the finnicky little things wrapped up so I can put this monster back together!
First of the finnickies is this little guy. It doesn't look like an issue here...
But touch it the wrong way and it falls right off. Thanks to whoever forced the back of the box open to pop the drawer, this needs to be reglued.
Which I did. And yes, I wiped the excess. I am not a lazybones.
With the repair work I could accomplish finished, I went around the cash box with a mixture of mineral spirits and tung oil to rehydrate the white oak and return some of the original shine. I opted this time around not to do any resurfacing or sanding, and rather spent some time picking the grime out of the routing. Honestly, despite the abuse, the wood was in pretty good condition. I let the surface cure for a week before running over it again, this time with a beeswax sealer. Something you might have noticed is that back panel laying on the top, and how the ends look like they're missing the same details I'd repaired along the side of the box. You'd be right, sadly. This back piece, which gives access to the back of the drawer and the bell assembly, had been physically pried off and shattered by some moron who wanted the drawer open after the register jammed. They didn't keep the busted pieces, because of course not why would you bother doing the absolute minimum, and I don't have the tools or woodworking experience to replicate what's missing. At some point I want to touch base with one of the cabinetry shops in town and have them recreate the end pieces for me, but for my own sanity I wanted to wrap this project up. Don't break antiques, kids. >:(
Polishing up the hardware for the drawer was a fun last minute thing, and I'll never not love the look of clean brass on antique oak. So good!
With the cash box finished, it's time to move back to the cash register mechanism. Namely, this grotey little keys! Bleugh. That's a lot of dried up finger juice.
Cleaning the key caps wasn't difficult, but it was S L O W. The gunk on them was rock hard and unwilling to budge without some mechanical assistance, so I had to break out the dental wax-up tool I keep handy to very gently scrape the crud away. Here you can see that I finished the left two columns, and the right two columns, leaving a stripe of grossness right down the middle. I have no idea why I did it this way. Again, I've been diagnosed with poobrain.
Badabing, nice and clean! Getting the same crud off the sides of the keys was more of a pain, which I didn't do a greeeat job documenting because I was so focused on getting it done. Suffice to say, cutting a few green scouring pads into strips and wiping them around the keys worked great. :D
Next, the steel hardware. The crank and the lever aren't plated in anything, and so they naturally take on a ruddy brown patina as they oxidize. I figured since I was already putting everything through the shiny-shiny process, I'd give both these pieces the wet-sand treatment to lift off the old rust and get them ready to showtime. While cast iron doesn't exactly have the same gleam as brass, it has its charm. :)
Same goes for the handle assembly, which I had to be a bit more careful with given I didn't want to introduce debris that would cause it to lock up. Since only the duckbill portion and its hinge would be visible after reassembly, I focused on cleaning just those sections.
After which, I gave the original marble slab a quick wash. (chefkiss.jpg)
Wiped down the decorative plates behind the glass windows.
Ran over the original glass with 0000 steel wool to remove the stuck on grime, then Windexed them til they were nice and clean.
Very carefully did the same with the window glass for all the tiny counters. These are only 1/16" thick, which makes handling them extra not stressful. :D
And finally I wrapped up making adjustments on the new, beveled window that I had cut for me to replace the one that the seller shattered during delivery. Unfortunately the place I go to for glass wasn't really set up to do their own beveling, since they order out through a third party for that. Rather than charge me a mint by going that route, their guy offered to do his best to match the original template with the tools they had. And honestly, he didn't to a bad job, he just cut the piece slightly too long for the socket it had to fit in. Given they only charged me $7, I wasn't going to go back and ask for him to redo it. So I set up a poor man's grinding station, hooked my vacuum's hose attachment to the bench vise, and very carefully ground down the long end with my stone wheel. The end result fit, as they say in the trades, good enough. :P
At which point I turned my attention to the absolute messes that were the red guards that pop up when a transaction is cycled. These pieces were shedding old paint like dandruff, the flakes of which were landing all over the internal mechanisms. I wasn't so crazy about that and decided to hit them with a fresh coat.
I knocked off all the old paint with, I kid you not, a plastic spatula. This stuff came off disturbingly easy.
Then I hit it with a rattlecan, picking a supergloss cherry red that's formulated to adhere to metal. Hooray for paint that etches as it cures! Boo for that can costing $15.
Also, booyeah for trash bags preventing any overspray from getting onto the numbered wheels!
Sure, the workshop smelled like spraypaint for most of a day, but that's what open windows and ceiling fans are for.
With nothing else to fix, tweak, paint, polish, or grind... it's time for the last step. Reassembly.
Do you know what's heavy? That whole f#($king mechanism is heavy. Seriously, taking it off was bad enough. Putting it back on and trying to line up the bolts with the holes in the frame is an absolute NIGHTMARE. Profanities were used. Backs were made to ache. I'm pretty sure I have permanent indents on my forearms thanks to that flesh-friendly frame. Next time I do one of these I'm getting a SMALL one. Also, notice that morning sunlight. Haha. Ah, yeah, morning. That's definitely when I finished this up.
Before any of the brass can go on, I have to reassemble all the small stuff I took off to repair the main mechanism. This little counter cover tends to sneak past me, so I tightened her down first.
Also, forgive me, but this register is a design I'm only barely familiar with so the reassembly process took a lot of focus to do. I didn't quite get photos of every step, so if you notice me jumping forward, that's kind of why. D: Sorry! Anyhow, this big block of cast iron goes on next. I swept it off, but since it's just framework I wasn't worried about it being a little ugly.
Same goes for these locks. So much of the plating had degraded that prettying them up wasn't something I was willing to spend the time or funds on. The only parts of them that are visible are the lock faces, so that's what I shined up. Everything else is hidden, and since they're functional, I considered that just fine.
Whabam, what'd I tell ya? Putting all the decorative stripwork (hehe) on took over an hour, and I'll tell you why. Each of those strips are held on by very tiny, very nonmagnetic screws that love nothing more than to be dropped by dumb internet weirdos like me. So, naturally, I KEPT DROPPING THEM. Seriously, I spent more time fishing screws out of the mechanism and crawling around the floor trying to find where they went than I spent actually tightening them down.
Still, I have to admit, it's still worth all the frustration. So shiny, so classy!
Finally, it's panel time. While we're on the topic of frustrating, finnicky bits of metal, something I didn't take any progress pictures of is that skinny lever sticking up out of the printer assembly. Why didn't I take any pictures? Because Ellie was strugglin'. That lever is connected to a pivot point you can't see, which has a teensy hole for a cotter pin to hold it down. I forgot the cotter pin ever existed, so for a shameful amount of time I kept staring at the old disassembly pictures wondering how the hell this thing went back in. It didn't help that during disassembly, I had been so focused on figuring out how to remove the lever that I took NO PHOTOS. I am poobrain. I no smort. Anyway, I figured it out. Eventually. Picture a chimpanzee working out the square peg in a square hole puzzle. That's me. Only the scientists would've shipped me off to the lipstick testing station way before I figured out how all the random parts I had lying around went together to make this lever assembly work.
Anyway! I remembered to screw down this receipt chute! So that's good! :D
Circling around, the rear panel snaps on without issue and I even managed to install the glass without confusing my primate brain!
At which point I utterly forgot to take pictures of the other side panel, because I was entirely focused on getting it to interface with the absolute carnival show that is the front panel. Because Ellie was tired and not very smart at this point. Mostly because of this little three-holed piece she didn't realize went here. In my defense, this bit was removed from the register when I bought it and came in a baggy with a bunch of other loose bits and bobs. I assumed it had something to do with the holes down and to the left. I was wrong, because I was dumbpilled.
Notice how the light looks a little less... morning-y. And more evening-y. Yeahhh... Anyhow, after figuring out that weird flanged piece, I remembered that I can't put together the front until I replace all the glass windows, the lock, and the locking mechanism inside this counter cover! Haha, hooboy I'm glad I caught that.
Looks pretty nice, all clean and intact again!
Whabam! We're going to pretend this portion of the resassembly didn't take two solid hours and just appreciate how glorious it's beginning to look. *sobs in unpaid work hours*
Popping the printer cover on felt good. Of course, now that I was really looking at it, I noticed that circular window at the top and realized... huh. Yeah, that's supposed to be a window. With glass. I need to order glass. Oh my god. FUTURE PROJECT. PRETEND ITS NOT THERE.
Oh look something different pay attention to this now haha! I don't have a spool of paper to fit this little note taking accessory, but I put out feelers with the greybeards in hopes they might have an idea. Honestly, I might be able to just make something. It'd be pretty neat to have this functional :)
I was able to recover the paper label the original owners used. Just a little detail I'm glad to have saved.
Popping in the marble really made this start to feel like I was nearing the finish line. How this slab survived without any chips, breaks, or permanent stains is beyond me and I'm not about to start borrowing trouble by thinking too hard about it.
Same goes for the original glass windows. How they all survived for 115 years without cracking is beyond me. It still bugs me that the person I bought it from caused the most damage by jamming this whole thing in the trunk of his sedan.
Popped the upper frame on, and the paper chute as well. That chute is held in by two pins about as thick as a paperclip, so I'm a little more than eager to get the paper reel hooked on to stabilize it.
That's better.
I saved the original paper spool and taped down the loose end to keep it as part of the register as a display. The printer might not ever work again, but it's still nice to see the register stocked up like it would've been when it was used.
Finally, the cap gets screwed down, and my weary brain forgets to take pictures of the serial number plate or the counter lid going on the front. xD Aw, man, I didn't even realize that until just now.
The Story of Judgement required attention, and that's the excuse I'm going to stick with. Anyway, the register is done, and it's time for some befores and afters!
Here it is, back when I first bought it in October of last year, hopelessly jammed, missing parts, heavily tinkered with, and damaged by the seller who at the very least drove it across the state to my house.
And here she is as of this morning, completely restored!
It's going to take a few days for me to really digest just how much work I put into this machine. I've had my face in this thing for months and somehow it feels like longer.
It ain't perfect, and it has plenty of battle scars to show, but it's an absolute beauty and I'd easily do it all over again.
At some point I do want to get that back board repaired by a professional, but for the time being it'll stay as-is. My brain needs time to decompress after spending so long on a single project.
Seeing how it looks in shadow really has me excited for what it could look like once the patina returns and the brass takes on a richer, darker color. For the immediate future, I don't think I'll be selling this one. Maybe later on, but this is a project that I think I'll sit on for a few years. I just need to find a piece of furniture I can put it on that isn't my coffee table.
Obligatory money shot to show all three mediums in close up. :)
I did make a point while I was repairing the mechanism to manually unlock the guard shield that keeps you from seeing the counters, solely so I could see the wheels after it was all put together. Just makes me happy to see them all peeking through. I've never had a register that didn't hide them under a lid or a cover!
I'd also like to make up some labels to put into the windows next to the lever. I have the originals, but they're so decayed that they break apart if I pick them up wrong. I'm hanging on to them for when I come up with something new, so I know the proper way to make them.
Look, I'm short. Any chance I get to make myself feel tall is a chance I'll take.
Seriously though, I'm beyond relieved to have this project done for now. I love doing these restorations but this one definitely took more mental energy than I'm used to, and I'm welcoming the break between this and the next project. For those who don't know, my other hobby is writing fiction and I'm looking forward to dedicating a lot more time toward that for a bit!
Of course, a project isn't done until the bell ringing is complete! You have no idea how buttery smooth that movement is. I have never once gotten a crank to turn over as nicely as this, which might be another reason I'm likely to keep this one for myself. :) And that's it! Three months of work and this mechanical beauty is ready to be put on display... somewhere in the house! Thank you again to all of you who have followed this project, given encouragement, offered solutions, or donated to my Kofi to help lift some of the financial burden. It's been a hell of a journey that I don't think I'd have done nearly as well with had it not been for you guys helping me along. As always, if you'd like to have a hand in this and future restorations, you can contribute here! ---> https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse Now I'm gonna put my shoes on and drive my happy ass down to Culver's for an end-of-project butterburger. While I do that, here's your moment of zen:
(Sauce: AnontheAnon) Thanks again, everyone!!
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse

item.title: Tavern's Closed
item.description: Everyone says making a living as comic artists is impossible. But it's not. Every time a reader decides to support our work by becoming a Patron, it gets us one step closer to our goal. Our goal to deliver that much needed comic relief. Spend a minute of your day with us, laugh with us, revel in the absurdity of the world with us. We promise to keep going, if you promise to keep reading – and supporting us. https://www.patreon.com/warandpeas
item.dc:creator: Warandpeasofficial

item.title: Nope, not making a unique title.

Curious little cat, understands zero to nothing. The cat just observes.
Elegant little kitty, can you be less cute please? A happy kitty, the winner has been decided.
item.dc:creator: Bruskey

item.title: Best spin-off series from a movie
item.dc:creator: ThailandExpress

item.title: Agent Orange
item.description: Some of you may have already heard of Agent Orange before, but I think not many has an idea on what it looks like. This is how it looks like. Last year I planned to cover this story in a form of documentary photographs, even mapped villages impacted by Agent Orange based on archived records found scattered online... but I had to cancel due to how costly it is. During the Vietnam War, the US military carried out a large scale herbicide campaign known as "Operation Ranch Hand." Millions of acres of farmland were sprayed. The result? The toxic chemicals contaminated the soil and river, leading to birth defects, cancer, immune disorders, among many other things across multiple generations. - Invisible Orders
item.dc:creator: unwaver

item.title: Sketchins'... IX
item.description: SOURCE: https://www.artstation.com/stato
item.dc:creator: stardustLUST

item.title: GovBrief Today #422
item.description: Eight million of you showed up today. People filled 3,300 events across all 50 states and around the world, with 200,000 in Minneapolis, where Bruce Springsteen performed "Streets of Minneapolis" about the two Americans ICE killed there in January. People marched because gas is up a dollar a gallon in one month, grocery prices keep climbing, and financial markets are reeling from a war nobody voted for. No one expects any single day of protest will create change. That's why today needs to be the continuation, not the finale. The war the United States started a month ago has already spread across eight countries. Today, 2,500 Marines arrived in the region. Reporting from multiple outlets says American ground forces will invade Iran. The midterms are important, but that's too long to wait for change. Every issue of GovBrief has a free toolkit you can use to help bring about change: Mobilize for local events, 5 Calls for reaching your lawmakers, and Resistbot for contacting your representatives by email and text. Make it part of your day. We've emailed 6,700+ stories over 422 straight nights. The algorithm doesn't always show them to you. Subscribe now at govbrief.today #GovBriefToday #Resist Subscribe (It's Free!) - https://gbounacos.substack.com Today on the Website: https://go.govbrief.today/260328-full [1] https://go.govbrief.today/iran-war-mar28 [2] https://go.govbrief.today/iran-ground-troops [3] https://go.govbrief.today/fbi-swalwell-files [4] https://go.govbrief.today/haiti-house [5] https://go.govbrief.today/tsa-callouts [6] https://go.govbrief.today/nokings-3 Subscribe (It's Free!) - https://gbounacos.substack.com
item.dc:creator: GeorgeBounacos
